title: The Unfinished Digital Revolution: Expanding Internet Access

hero.title: The Unfinished Digital Revolution: Expanding Internet Access
hero.lead: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed, yet digital opportunity remains deeply unequal. While networks now reach much of the world, millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy. The reasons go beyond infrastructure, and reflect gaps in affordability, skills, and inclusive policy design.
hero.byline: May 2026, Story by Ana Florina Pirlea & Lorena Corso, Visuals by Jan Willem Tulp & Maarten Lambrechts, Illustrations: Drew Bardana

keyFacts.label: people worldwide do not use the Internet.
in Sub-Saharan Africa are not covered by a 5G network.
of people in low-income countries own a smartphone.

blocks.title: Global Internet access: trends and divides
blocks.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population)

scenes.text: [emphasis: Worldwide], the share of people who access the Internet has increased from 16 percent only two decades ago to over 70 percent today. Still, over a quarter of the global population (2.2 billion) remains offline.

Internet adoption mirrors an economy’s level of development: it is nearly universal (94 percent) in [emphasis: high-income countries] but less than a quarter (23 percent) in [emphasis: low-income countries].
Basic Internet access is lowest in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], where only around one-third of the population is online.

blocks.html: Access is not only about national averages. Where people live — cities versus rural areas — still shapes whether they can get online, especially in lower-income economies.

blocks.title: The urban–rural digital divide
blocks.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet, by place of residence (% of population)

scenes.text: Across every income group, urban residents are more likely to use the Internet than people in rural areas. The gap is widest in low-income economies.

blocks.html: Gender compounds the divide. In poorer economies, women are far less likely than men to use the Internet.

blocks.title: Gender gaps in Internet access are wide in low- and lower-middle-income economies
blocks.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population), by gender and income level

scenes.text: In low-income economies the gender gap exceeds 10 percentage points. In high-income economies male and female use are nearly equal — both near universal.

blocks.html: Since 2015 almost every economy has moved forward — but the speed of progress varies widely.

blocks.title: Almost all economies have increased their Internet access since 2015
blocks.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population)
blocks.source: ITU DataHub and World Bank staff calculations.

scenes.text: In 2015, many countries still had low Internet use. Each dot shows a country’s starting point.
By 2024, in 54 countries more than 90 percent of the population was using the Internet. Most economies moved right — higher access — but the distance covered varies widely.
Countries like [emphasis: Cambodia] and the [emphasis: Kyrgyz Republic] made much more progress than expected from their 2015 levels.
Explore all countries. Highlight any economy to compare its path and the speed of progress between 2015 and 2024.

blocks.html: How people connect has shifted. Mobile broadband now dwarfs fixed lines almost everywhere outside the richest economies.

blocks.title: Worldwide, mobile broadband far surpasses fixed broadband adoption
blocks.subtitle: Subscriptions per 100 people

scenes.text: Between 2015 and 2025, fixed broadband roughly doubled to about 20 subscriptions per 100 people, while [emphasis: mobile connectivity surged toward 99 active mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people].
[emphasis: Fixed broadband] is common in many high-income countries — for example Germany has roughly 46 fixed subscriptions per 100 people.
Fixed broadband is nearly absent in many low and lower-middle-income economies. Highlighted countries sit near the bottom of the fixed axis.
Across much of the world, people connect primarily through mobile phones — including in Morocco, Côte d’Ivoire, Senegal, Nigeria, Kenya and Ghana.

blocks.html: Network coverage is not the same as use. Most people now live within range of a mobile signal — but generations differ, and Sub-Saharan Africa lags on 5G.

blocks.title: Most of the world is covered by 3G and 4G networks. Sub-Saharan Africa is the least connected region
blocks.subtitle: Percentage of inhabitants within range of at least a 3G, 4G, or 5G signal

scenes.text: 96 percent of the global population lives within reach of at least a 3G network. Ranked bars show countries with the lowest coverage first.
Although 4G covers over 93 percent of people worldwide, large gaps remain in low-income economies — especially rural Sub-Saharan Africa.
5G is widely available in many high-income economies and some large middle-income countries such as China, but still uneven globally.
In Sub-Saharan Africa, 5G remains largely confined to a few high-density urban areas; only a handful of economies (e.g. Mauritius, South Africa) show meaningful coverage.

blocks.html: Even with coverage, speed determines what the Internet is good for — streaming, cloud work, education platforms.

blocks.title: High-income economies enjoy high-speed Internet
blocks.subtitle: Median country download speeds (Mbps)
blocks.source: Speedtest / Ookla public country medians (via Atlas data).

scenes.text: Top fixed and mobile download speeds are concentrated in high-income and East Asian economies. The two panels rank the fastest countries for fixed broadband and mobile.

blocks.html: Coverage without adoption leaves people offline. Where 3G reaches most of the population but Internet use stays low, the barrier is affordability, skills, devices — not towers alone.

blocks.title: Mobile networks now reach most of the population, but many people remain offline
blocks.subtitle: 3G coverage vs Internet use (% of population)
blocks.source: ITU coverage and Internet use statistics.

scenes.text: Each slope links a country’s 3G coverage to its Internet use. Red slopes highlight the largest coverage–use gaps — networks exist, but people stay offline.

blocks.html: Basic digital skills still lag. In many countries a large share of people have never sent a text message — a proxy for very limited mobile literacy.

blocks.title: In 27 countries, more than 50% of people have never sent a text message
blocks.subtitle: Share of respondents who can send a text message through a mobile phone
blocks.source: Gallup / World Bank digital skills surveys (Atlas data).

scenes.text: Values show the share who can send a text. Red bars mark countries where more than half the population has never sent one (capability below 50%).

blocks.html: Finally, devices. Without a smartphone, modern apps, banking, and learning platforms stay out of reach — especially in low-income countries.

blocks.title: Few people in low-income countries own a smartphone
blocks.subtitle: Share of people who report that their main mobile phone is a smartphone
blocks.source: Gallup World Poll / Atlas digital inclusion indicators.

scenes.text: The share of people who do not own a smartphone is highest in poorer countries — approaching 80 percent in some low-income economies.
Among low-income countries, places such as Malawi, Burkina Faso, Chad, and the DRC show particularly high shares without a smartphone.

blocks.html:
Networks now reach much of the world, yet millions remain offline. Closing the gap takes more than towers: affordability, skills, devices, and inclusive policy all matter.
